Okay, we're going to talk about the kinds of deals that you can structure with a client. The objective here is not necessarily to maximize, to get every penny you can out of every job. Marcella made the point today that she's made a lot of money with Motley Fool because she gave away a package at one point. So this is about win-win deals. What you're trying to do is to create a long-term relationship with a client. And I always forget what the next slides are, but I'm going to say this anyway now. We just breeze over it later if we come to a slide. When you're talking to a client about renewal campaigns, the theory is that he should be willing to spend as much to renew a customer as he did to get that customer in the first place or to save a customer who's trying to cancel or get a refund as he paid to get that customer in the first place. It's easier to save that customer, typically, cheaper to save that customer than it is to go find a new one. So the same is true with your clients. You get a job with a Motley Fool. That was a life-changing experience from Marcella. If she had let that lie, she might not have got another assignment from them, although I think she probably would have because they loved her copy and they felt they understood that it was the theme that they had all bought into that really killed the promotion. But keeping a client is the key here, right? So they're willing to do a thing with you and you've got to structure your deal now. This way you don't go for every penny. You don't get greedy. You make it clear that you're there for the client. You're their champion. You're their advocate. Your passion, your life's purpose is to get them more customers or to get more money out of their house file for them. So that is the feeling that you need to be creating as you're talking with the customer. How can I best help you kind of attitude? So your objectives here are get great assignments. Yes. Get paid. Yes. Get paid well. Yep. Not necessarily every dime you can squeeze out of the client. Paid well and fairly. But the most important thing is to set the groundwork, set the foundation for future assignments. I'm going to go through some stuff here that sounds really good coming from me in my position today, 42 years into this career, but maybe more difficult for people who are just getting started who can't afford to be quite as picky. But at some point in your career you need to be at the point where you are picky about your assignments. It's all part of maximizing your income, remember. Good assignments. So we're going to approach each assignment a little bit skeptically. We're going to ask some tough questions. How long do I think this assignment is going to take me? Is this a two month project? Is it a two week project? Is it a two day project? The answer to that is going to have a lot to do with what your rates are and what kind of deal you structure. What is the revenue potential for the client on this project? Is this a gold mine for him? You want to look at the product. How strong a product is it? How unique is it? What kind of success stories and testimonials do you have indicating that it's a good product? If you're being given a theme, are you totally on board with it? I had a, Wiley publishers came to me and said, we want you to write a book. And I said, okay, I'll write a book. And they said, we want it to be on writing to human nature. And I said, nah, I don't think so. That's not me. That's Daniel Levine, with Levis. Levis. Daniel Levis. And that's not me. You should talk to him. So I walked away from a nice healthy advance and the opportunity to be published by Wiley because I didn't like the theme. If you don't believe in the theme you're being given, then you need not necessarily walk away, but that's on the negative side of your little roster here, your little list. Market size, okay? If this is just being sold to his list and he only has 10,000 customers, the revenue potential is not very large. If it's being sold on the outside and he only has a $5,000 a month media budget, this thing is not going to generate much in terms of revenue or royalties for you. See where we're going with this. And then the deal itself. The smaller the market, the bigger your deal has to be, right? Or the bigger your royalty or the bigger the front end or whatever. So you need to take all of these things into account as you're evaluating a project that you've been offered. When you ask yourself, does the revenue potential to me, right? Justify my investment in time and effort in this project. And again, the slide we just looked at, those are the things that will help you determine whether or not this is a high potential job. If the answer is no, it doesn't mean the deal's necessarily dead. You can ask yourself, how can I sweeten the pot? For example, you could say, this is not going to give me big royalties, but what if we did this as a serial assignment where I have to do one of these every quarter for you and you pay me on a monthly basis? Or I want the old SRC story for me. I had written a direct mail package that took SRC to the moon, security record to the moon, and I just asked one day, hey, could I see what you use? To sell these coins to your customers. So you can ask your client, well, I know you want this project, but what are you doing to generate leads or convert leads? Or what are you doing to your house file? And look for another project in there that you can use. You could also recommend that your client go to the idea of affiliate marketing. Believe it or not, a lot of people still don't know what that is. And you basically say, look, I'll do this, but we need a bigger universe. So let's go to some of your competitors and offer them half the money in return for sending this promotion to their file. Look for ways to make the pie bigger. I had taken a safe money report to 120,000 paid subs, and I was getting paid 50 bucks a thousand for direct mail. And so we're mailing three million pieces a month, so I was making some pretty good money. But Dan Rosenthal, that dirty dog, had come in and done a deal where he was doing just the back end marketing, and he was getting 30% of revenues. And the client would rather slash his own wrist than to tell me this, right? Now this really doesn't have to do, well, it does. It has to do with making the deal sweeter. So I said to the client, look, basically what's happening here is I'm doing the heavy lifting, the hard work, selling to cold prospects, and I'm bringing in thousands and thousands of new customers. And I'm getting 50 bucks a thousand for doing that. And then Dan Rosenthal does the easy stuff, which is selling stuff to existing customers. He's getting 30% of your revenues. So I'm bringing them in, Dan's creaming them, making millions. This cannot stand. So my client says, well, what do you want? And I said, I want an overwrite on Dan's commission. If I'm bringing this guy in, I want a piece of the action on the back end, too. And so now all of a sudden I'm getting 10% of the revenues on the back end. How to make four million a year, okay? I was making four million a year with this deal, because we were doing 10, 12 million, 30 million some years on the back end. 10% goes to Clayton, and then I was getting all the royalties on the front end. So make the pie bigger, look for opportunities. He's got a front end, he's got a back end. He asked you to write for the front end, get a piece of the back end, and vice versa. This is where you put on your negative hat, okay? Your Martin Weiss hat, okay? Where you look for every possible problem, everything that could go wrong, every eventuality of Murphy's law, okay? Is the product proven? No? Okay, that's a risk factor. We don't know if the market will like this product or hate this product. Is this particular market proven? And by market, I simply mean the people you're going to be talking to. It could be a house list, like Kim Githler's house list. She has 400,000 names, like I told you yesterday, only 70,000 are opted into marketing. And none of those people are bonded to the company, right? That's her market, unproven, big risk, okay? Unproven media, say for example, your client has been doing only direct mail, and he wants you to do a web based promotion. But he has no experience in that area. He doesn't have any knowledge of the lists that are available. He doesn't have any knowledge of the websites where he can place ads. The people behind the scenes are the most important people in the world at that point. He may or may not even have people on his staff that have the technical expertise to do this work. An unproven theme, almost everything you do is going to be an unproven theme, but it is a risk factor. An unknown refund rate. A lot of your royalties will be based on net of 30 day refunds. So if you get, let's say you sell a million dollars worth of something, you have a 10% royalty on that, so you get $100,000 royalty, but they're not going to pay you right away, right? They're going to wait 30 days and see how many of those people cancel. If 30% of them cancel, now your royalty is only 70,000 instead of 100,000. So if it's a product that's like, and this is where the right triumphs, okay? If it's a crappy product, you're going to get a high refund rate, okay? So you're rewarded for selecting only good products. Only products that bring real value to people's lives, okay? So don't think that you're going to get away with it, because you will pay the price in somewhere or another if you decide to go ahead and promote a product you don't believe in. Response time, and for everybody that's done in this room who's ever done work for Weiss Research, I apologize. Our production department is so freaking slow. I'm in charge of strategy and copy, right? Execution. The people in charge of hiring and firing are over in Jupiter, Florida. And my god, if any of you think that you've been waiting a long time, like you, Chris, to see your copy out on the web. I wrote stuff in October that still hasn't seen the light of day, and one of those projects has actually been lost. They can't find it. So you gotta know how, ask the question, how long from the moment I turn in the final will we have this up on the web? In a direct mail, one of the things that the gurus and stuff often fail to mention is somebody calls you today to write a direct mail piece. It's gonna be a couple of weeks until you're actually ready to start writing. It may take four weeks to write it or longer. After you write it, it has to go through compliance. It has to be laid out in production. The lists have to be rented. It could be two months before, easily two months before your copy sees the light of day. Then once it sees the light of day, it's gonna be at least another two weeks until they get to their doubling date. You guys know what the doubling date is? Doubling date is, a mailer has done a whole lot of mailings and he knows at what point after the drop, at what point after the drop, he has received 50% of his total revenue for that promotion. So if a promotion is supposed to bring in a million dollars, he may find out for example that 14 days after he drops the mailing, he usually has half his money from that promotion. He then uses that number. He looks at day 14, he looks at his results, doubles the amount of money that's come in, doubles the number of responses. And at that point, knows whether you have the new control or not. And he has to do it early because the more mailings he does in a year, the more money he makes, right? So rather than wait for six weeks for the mailing to run its total cycle, two weeks in now he can make a go and no go decision. And start running lists and start doing the other things he has to do in order to get the roll out ready. So that's a doubling date. Online your doubling date is five seconds after the promotion goes out. And that's kind of painful too, as Marcel pointed out this morning sometimes. And then collection, and this is where, when do they pay? I've walked into deals, eyes wide open, signed the contracts, don't really read them, just kind of quick scan them. Check to make sure the royalty rates right, the upfront payment rate is right. Sign on the dotted line, send it back in, write the copy. Yay, I got a winner, big winner, fantastic. Next month, where's my money? We only pay semi-annually. Or quarterly on royalties. No, no, no, no. Be sure to check that in a contract. You want to be paid on the 15th of the month following the month in which the money was made. Typically. Now if they're paying you net of cancels, of course it would be. Add one month to that. Also, any of you write for night in Gale Conant at all? Okay. Okay, well, I did a Deepak Chopra promotion for them. And I saw them mail that thing for two years and I never saw a single royalty. Now, for all I know. Well, they paid me, my contract demanded that they pay me. By the way, if somebody tells you they don't pay royalties, do not believe them. Rodeo Press sent out this big letter to all of their, this is prevention magazine, men's health magazine, doctor's book, home remedies. So they sent out this email to all of the copywriters that were working for them. And they said, Rodeo will no longer pay royalties. We're just not going to do it. So just deal with it, right? A minute after that thing went out, Pat Corpers on the phone to me going, they weren't talking about you. Yeah, so they were getting all these other copywriters going, man, I want to work for prevention and I probably will anyway, but no royalty. But it was a lie, they were just trying to cheap out on the copywriters. So collection, another part of that too. You can always, especially if it's a named client, you can always call or email one of us or AWI and see what the scuttlebutt is. Do they pay, do they pay on time? Do they honor their contracts? Here's a tough question, am I really the right writer for this job? Do I have the time to do this right? Especially when you're young in this business, the last thing you want to do is rush through a job. I mean, it's a deadly mistake, you need to allow plenty of time. So do I have the time to do this right? Do I have the resources to do this right? Is this going to require that I do the research or find a researcher? If so, think about that, how am I going to do that? Do I have enough experience in this niche? One of the most uncomfortable situations you can find yourself in is to be writing a financial promotion, something about options, for example, which is very, very complex, when you know nothing about options. You can probably fake yourself through a simple stock promotion, just kind of tease these great companies, the guy is recommended, don't name them, offer them a free report that names them, tout his track record, you don't need to know a lot about investment to do that. Quick tip, if you're going to write for finance, first thing you do is take an amount of money that would be painful to lose and invest it. Open a Fidelity account online, if $10,000 is your number, put it in there. An amount that if you had to tell your spouse that you lost it all, you probably wouldn't speak to each other for a week or two, a painful amount, right? And invest it. You will be amazed at how interesting the investment news becomes, instantaneously, how you're just riveted by every story on CNBC and the daily closing of the Dow Jones and the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ. Have a stake in the game, okay? So adequate experience with the niche, yes or no? What is adequate? I'm a brand new writer Clayton, what do you talk about adequate experience with the niche? Well, it could just be an understanding of the niche. Maybe you're a customer of some of these vitamin companies, maybe you're a private investor and so you have some experience, you know how things work. And do I have adequate experience with this kind of strategy? Now again, this is not necessarily just experience for the people who are new to this, it could be knowledge. And so if the guy comes to you says, we wanna do a product launch strategy campaign for XYZ, you go, I got you, I know what that is. I've done the work, I have that skill. So depending on your answers, you wanna consider your options. Would it be possible to turn the air down a little, get a little cooler in here? You need to consider your options. Now, you may wanna ask for an adjustment in the schedule. Okay, let's, no, I really can't get it done in two weeks and do a real good job for you. I'll need three or four or whatever it is. Be honest up front. It's a living hell when the client is calling you every day and saying, where is my ethane copy? Which Richard Stanton Jones used to do to me all the time. He's this very reserved Brit. But if you were a week late on copy, you would get this call and you'd be screaming. I'm not gonna copy. So, and so you start not answering the phone and dreading, staring at it, knowing it's gonna ring at any minute. Identify the resources you need and go get them. Consider drafting a copy chief. You know, all of us are quite busy, but from time to time, we have openings in our schedule. But also at boot camp, you meet a lot of other copywriters. Really great ones, John Ford comes to mind. You know, Herschel, you know. And maybe some of those guys would be from time to time willing to just look at your copy for 500 bucks or something like that. You're getting a 10,000, $12,000 to write it. Investing a thousand of that to have a real pro look at your copy and give you pointers is certainly worth the money. Consider partnering with another writer who does have experience in the niche or with the strategy, you know, or maybe even with this very product. Now you can decide to learn on the job, but allow for that. You know, if it's gonna take you more time to learn stuff about options investing, for example, build it into the schedule. Or you could walk away. Now, short of walking away, you can go to the client, you know, in this early process where you're talking. This isn't after you accept the job, of course. So you're still in the early process. You go to the client and say, you know what? I mean, man, I would love this if a copywriter did this to me. You know, I really would like to write this package, but I just don't think I'm the right person for this job. I don't know this subject area or whatever. But you have this other product that I'm just dying to write for, you know? And now there's nothing more attractive to somebody who hires a copywriter. Then they have a copywriter passionate about one of his products. You know? That passion will take you a long way. And so you say, you know, I've been dying to write for this. I really think I can beat your control. I can't, I really shouldn't do this, to be fair to you, you know? Now, what's that gonna do to him, to that client? He's just gonna thank the world of you for doing that. No one else has ever done that to him, you know? They just take what they can get. And then they'll blunder through it and fail and he'll be out the money and the time and the opportunity cost and everything else. So consider that as well. All right, now, I'm glad we're not doing this after lunch because this is a lot of left brain stuff and I'd have a room full of sleeping people. But there are some, and we're gonna cover more later in this presentation, but there are some numbers that you need to be aware of. You need to be able to look at a promotion the way your client looks at a promotion. Okay? And so he's gonna be using acronyms as he's talking about the results of your promotion. One is ROI, what's return on investment, okay? This is a very important number because especially in direct mail, they manage by this number. Before your promotion, before the lists are selected for your promotion, the client has a number in his mind, an ROI number, okay? And typically it'll be 100% or somewhere around 100%. And what that means is for every dollar he puts into the mail in postage costs, printing costs, and all the rest, list rental, so forth. He wants $1 back. Now, you think about it, it's technically a loss to him, right? Because he has to fulfill that product now. So if it's a newsletter, it's probably gonna cost him $15, $20 over the period of a year to fulfill that subscriber. And he's willing to do that out of his own pocket. But in terms of the mailing costs themselves, he wants 100% ROI. Now, depending on what's going on in the world, he may slide that as low as 85% of ROI if he's got a real hot winner. Or if he's desperate, either way. He may say, you know what, this is working so well, or so poorly. I'm willing to take a loss on the front end. But I still need the new customers. After 9-11, we raised our ROI target to 120% because there was risk in the marketplace. So how do you do that? Well, he has a list history and he knows how every mailing list he's ever mailed to is performed on average. And so he knows the average ROI over time for every list he's got. And it's a ballpark number because he's used different promotions or seasonality and other things in there. But he knows if he mails to list A, he's likely to get on average an ROI of around 105%. So then if he wants to hit 100% ROI for the total mailing, he'll say, okay, here's the deal. In descending order, these are my most responsive lists. And there's a cumulative ROI column. Does this make sense to you? I'm talking about your right brain people, about a left brain thing. So it's, but there's a cumulative ROI column. He just simply looks down that column, takes a red pencil, red lines, the last list where the cumulative ROI hits 100% if that's his target. If he wants 120%, the red line's up here somewhere, okay? So, and usually the only reason, I told one of my marketing managers, the only thing you can do wrong here is make a profit on an acquisition mailing. If you make a profit on an acquisition mailing, you'll probably be fired. I would rather lose money, lose a little bit. So always be more aggressive. Cuz if you make a profit, that means you didn't mail enough pieces, which means I didn't get enough customers as many as I could have. So that's ROI. CPL is cost per lead, pretty straightforward. I put $1000 out there on the website. It works in Direct Mail too, cuz there are plenty of direct mail based lead production campaigns. But I put $1000 out there on the web to buy media, to drive traffic to my squeeze page, and a certain number of people will squeeze and I'll get that lead. So it's simply the amount of money I spent divided by the number of leads I got. And right now a really good cost per lead would be around five bucks or less. I mean, five is really good, anything less than that is spectacular. I pay at the beginning of a new campaign where we're still testing media and copy together, I may end up paying $23, $24 a lead. But that's unacceptable over the long haul. And by selecting better media, better targeted media and so forth, and sharpening the message over time, our goal is to get it down into that $5, $6 range, okay? Now there are a lot of different kinds of projects you'll be approached on. One is customer list promotions. What are the characteristics there? Well, he didn't have to rent a list or pay for media because he owns those names, right? So there's no media cost. It's low risk because the people know him and like him. They've had a good experience with him. The response rate is likely to be quite high. Average unit of sale and revenue are going to be quite high because the people trust him, they'll tend to go for the two year subscription more often. So lower refund rate, again, given a good product, lower refund rate, because again, there's a relationship there. And these campaigns are the clients bread and butter. This is what pays the salaries, pays the overhead. He doesn't make a dime. If he's doing it right, he doesn't make a dime on acquisition. This is how he pays the bills, okay? So the best practice right now is a fee plus a commission of about 5%. And the fee that I pay, the fee that Stansberry pays is $12,500. So if you work for me, I'm going to give you half of that when you say yes, I'll do the job. And I'll give you the other half when we accept your copy. And there's a whole plan for if we don't accept your copy. And we'll talk about that in the next session. All right, the next one is lead acquisition, right? This is where you just go out, all you want is an email address of somebody who's interested in something that you do in your niche, okay? So there's significant media cost. You're not talking to customers, so he has to rent a list. He has to pay to put a banner on a website, okay? It's much higher risk because these people don't know you from Adam. So response rates are very, very low. And there's no revenue. They're going to worry about monetizing these names later on. So the thing that they're looking at is cost per lead again, okay? Here, basically, it's the same front end, because it's typically very close to the same amount of work. $12,500 is best practice. And the royalty is anywhere from 5% to 10% of the CPL, the cost per lead. So if you have a $5 cost per lead, and quite often it'll be a sliding scale, the lower the cost per lead, the larger your percent, right? So you're incentivized. So if you have a $5 cost per lead, you might be getting 50 cents per lead. So you can see how that would add up, you bring in 100,000 leads, right? So that's lead acquisition. Then we have customer acquisition. These are the kinds of campaigns that up until two years ago, were bringing us tons of new customers. Somebody mentioned End of America, which was a video sales letter that Porter Stansbury did. We had a similar one, and we brought in 150,000 paying customers at $39 a pop with that. So we were making money on our acquisition campaigns, which is unheard of, very different kind of acquisition campaign. Obviously not like direct mail. But we did very well with it. More recently though, those kinds of direct to sale campaigns have worked less and less well, so we're doing more of the lead production. But if they ask you to produce a customer for them online, so now you're not just doing the drivers to a squeeze page, you're doing the drivers to a sales page. Maybe they squeeze first, but ultimately they're gonna end up on a sales page. So now you got a couple of things happening, right? But anyway, so the best practice here is fee plus 10% to 20% of revenues. Now, let's think about that for a minute, okay? I'm spending 300,000 a month to buy media to acquire customers. You come to me and say you want 20% of revenues. That means that I'm gonna be paying you that extra money instead of putting it in a media. So just getting the higher percent does not necessarily mean you're gonna make more money. You need to look at this very critically and analytically. An extra 5% may or may not affect the number of names they can drive to your sales page. It will to some degree. And direct mail certainly makes a big difference. The higher your royalty, that royalty comes right out of his ROI. So he can mail fewer pieces and still accomplish his goals. So just kind of keep that in mind, or at least understand that dynamic, okay? So those are the three kinds of deals that you're typically gonna see out here. They're also combined, and we were kind of going in that direction, combined campaigns where you produce a lead and then immediately convert that lead. And if you're doing that, you need to ask, and a client may or may not volunteer this, if there's a squeeze page anywhere in this process, as you're driving to the sales page, now he's getting a lead and he's getting some customers. So you deserve double compensation. And if you ever tell Martin Wise, I told you that. I will call you a liar. But it's really true, think about it. If you're doing just leads, you get a percentage of lead cost. If you're doing just sales, you get a percentage of sales. So why wouldn't you get a percentage of both on a combined campaign, right? Okay, now, most of the guys you talk to are going to have a standard payment plan, the bigger companies for sure. The smaller ones may not. So if a guy doesn't tell you, well, here's what we pay, right? What are you gonna do? How are you gonna set your fee? And the only advice I can give you is to just look at your own situation. What's your overhead? How much do you need? How long is this project gonna take? If it's a one month project and your overhead is $10,000 a month, you need $10,000 as an upfront fee, right? If you have other sources of income and you don't have that high a need for cash right now, you can accept a lower fee if you want to to get the job. But think of it in terms of your needs. What do you need to keep the lights on, keep the rent paid, and all of those other things? Worst thing in the world is you sitting there trying to write copy and the phone's ringing with bill collectors wanting their money. That's a nightmare. Again, with established companies, there may be some room for negotiation. Certainly with Marcella there is because we've expanded the job tremendously. And but usually not a lot. I've got a lot of, I get 16 copywriters on my hit list right now. Calvin's one of them, Marcella's one of them, and a bunch of other guys. And so I don't need a lot of new copywriters right now for this kind of work anyway. And so I can pretty much say, guys, this is what we pay and if you don't like it, there's a highway. The fact of the matter is that most of us talk to each other, as I said, highly incestuous. And so I know what Porter Stansbury, and now on my side, it's important to me to be able to get the best copywriters. That means I gotta pay for it. So I'm gonna be fair and I'm gonna be competitive, right? But then as you go on in your career, the more successes you create, you gain power. And with that power, you can demand pretty much you can write your own check. When that happens to you, and it will happen to a bunch of you guys in this room, Gary Benzavenga exercises that power in one of the nicest ways possible. He simply goes to the client and says, I'd really like to give you my best ideas. But I really can't do that under the current terms. I have to give them to other clients who'll pay more. What a great way to put it, right? So keep that in mind. All right, so deal for every situation. We're gonna look at all the different ways that people do deals. I did this at boot camps, so we'll go through it pretty quickly. There's free with contingency, that's what I did with Johnny Johnson. I don't even want to get paid. I'll beat your control by two to one, and then if I do, you give me this money. Fee only, there are, if you are at a place where you have to take the work, and you're basically a commodity, then fee only is fine. I know some, what's your name? Chris Marlow, Chris Marlow? She and I had a very public fight or debate over this. It was a fight? Yeah, I think it was. My philosophy is, I'm selling you a chunk of my life. I value my life, okay? Why would I settle for the lowest bid? Right? Why would I write for a fee only? Why wouldn't I at least strive to get a royalty? A shoe salesman gets a commission. Why can't I have a commission, right? And now she deals with people that work for Apple and they're not even real copywriters. I mean, they're not doing direct response copy. They're not being hired and fired based on results. And so she told me that I was crazy and deluded and everything by telling my people, look, demand a royalty. It's a commission, that's what salesmen get, we're salesmen. So I would try to avoid the fee only. You may have situations where you have to take it. Having said that, Jim Rutz, he's really big on missions and missionary stuff. And he'll take a break, a six month break from sometime. He'll disappear, he'll be in the wilds of Africa or New Zealand or someplace. And then he'll come back and he'll do some work. And last time he came back, when I talked to him, he had decided he didn't want royalties anymore. He wanted to be a fee only copywriter. The fee was $100,000. And I think I heard of him getting $200 on one job. So he had the power, he didn't want to worry about collecting royalties over time or monitoring the client because he was going to be outside of the country. He said, sure, come you check and I'll write your package for you. Yeah, Marcella? Okay, at the risk of starting a public brawl with Clayton. Let me just offer a counter position here, which is when I left Shaffers and I went out on my own, Bernie has always had the policy he will never pay royalties period. Clayton could go on and on about why that's shooting himself at the phase and blah, blah, but that was just it. He would not pay royalties. He did, he did. He would have offered you his first, second, third form child and everything else thrown in to get you to come work for him. But here's how you do the fee only. Okay, so when I left Shaffers, he wanted to pay me hourly, right? I did the math that Clayton was saying, I calculated, well, what in my old salary was I technically earning per hour, what would that translate to in an hourly rate? Try to figure out what I could ask for, because now he didn't have to pay my benefits, I had to pay my benefits, all of that. But here's how this helped me and actually saved my career. So I had started, when I left Shaffers, I started working with Paris as an apprentice. And when you work as an apprentice, you're gonna work a ton of hours for less money than you would make working at Starbucks. And you are only gonna get a little piece of the royalty because what you're really doing is getting an education, right? Remember when I said this is an old school industry, and you're paying to get an education? Yes, exactly. Okay, I mean, there's people, Clayton's got people who write and who say, let me just come work for you for three months, you'd have to pay me anything, all right? So I'm working with Paris, he's at least paying me something, but it's not enough to keep the lights on. So what I did is I figured out what I needed hourly, and that's what I billed Shaffers for. So Shaffers kept the lights on while I built a royalty business. Because the thing about royalties is one, you are not always going to get them, right? Everyone has a win rate. In the beginning, my win rate was not attractive, okay? And Paris's win rate is 80%. So Paris will book a royalty or more, I think. He'll book a royalty deal all day long. He knows he's gonna get them, all right? I didn't know if I was gonna get them or not. So I can't always count that I'm gonna get the royalties, okay? And the amount of hours I had to put in up front on those projects, because I'm still learning, and I can't write a boardroom package as fast as he can write it, meant that even if they paid me a gazillion dollars up front, I was still earning less than I would earn, making mocha lattes at Starbucks. So for me, doing the fee only hourly kept my lights on while I built my royalty based business. Which is what I said. And keep going. You're just beginning life and you need the money. Take the fee, the straight fee based job. It was a bridge for Marcella. It was a bridge for you. And I totally agree with that. But the fight I had with Marlowe was she was encouraging people to build a career on that. And those are the copywriters who if you go to Yahoo and you look up average compensation for a copywriter, it'll say 60,000 a year. That's who those people are. We're shooting for six figures as chump change, right? We're shooting for a lot more than that. So if you wanna do fee based, that's fine. If you wanna get a job with Porter Stansberry, that's fine. Spend a year, you'd go to college for four years or 12 years to become a doctor, right, to become a doctor. I think it's 12 years, right? And you make more money than a doctor when you do this right, right? So what's wrong with spending one year working for Porter Stansberry under Mike Palmer, writing copy every day, getting the best in the business, creating your copy? Now that's a straight fee, you know? Actually, they do give you the opportunity to earn some royalty, sir. But the education is worth it. So fee based, fee only, it's a bridge. It's a temporary condition at best. The minute you have some winners under your belt, you have a little bit of power with your clients you wanna move on. Okay, fee plus incentive, let's look at some of the different ways you can incentivize a copywriter. Royalty on impressions, in other words, for every thousand eyeballs that look at my sales page, I'm going to get this much money. So this would be dollar amount on impressions. In direct mail, it's a dollar amount per thousand pieces mailed. I don't know many people who are using this approach online. But in direct mail, if a guy mails a thousand pieces of my package, I get 50 bucks, right? So I know it doesn't sound much, but when you're mailing three million a month, it adds up. Commission on revenues, this is the shoe salesman commission, right? This is mostly online. The reason things are so different online offline, if I do a direct mail piece, my client has to pay $600, $700 a thousand to send that out to prospects. I write something online and it goes to his house file, for example. He pays nothing to send that out, right? If it goes to other leads online, the lead cost is much lower than direct mail. So that's why there are kind of two models here. Okay, the other one is sliding scale on cost per lead, okay? I'm gonna pay you 50 cents for every $10 lead you bring in. I'm gonna pay you a dollar for every $6 lead or $5 lead you bring in, okay? So you bring my lead cost down, and how do you do that? More effective copy, you know? He puts a thousand bucks out there, the more effective copy is gonna bring more people in, okay? That drives the cost per lead down. So the sliding scale compensates you for doing a better job. A bonus on the overall result. This is how I'm working now. I get a very high retainer, $100,000 a month. And then at the end of the year, we add up all the money that we brought in, and I get a nice big chunk of that money. And it's simple, you don't always have to be calculating and monitoring. And plus the upfront is big enough that I don't have to worry about income. And could I make more money with another deal? Absolutely. I could make a lot more money if I was 10% of sales each month, right? But this is steady work. I don't ever have to worry about having a client or having work to do, or, we'll talk more about that dynamic in a minute too. And then overwrite, I talked about that. You do a promotion for one part of the guy's business, say acquisition. And then you say, I wanna do that, I want this compensation for that, I also want a percentage of sales on the back end. Very rarely, you can get that, but I have gotten it. So I'm just trying to give you all of the moving parts here so you can structure the right kind of machine for you. There's a retainer deal, which is more what I'm on, right? And retainer plus incentive. Retainer would be really, you go to work for Bernie Schaefer for basic fee, right? It's essentially what it is for a basic salary. And then partnership, and this is how cool this business is. You can be as poor as a church mouse. You can start writing copy. You can work your way up. You can get yourself to a point through the exercise of your own brilliance, your own skills, your own tools. You can get to a point where you can just say, look, I'll write for you, but I want half your company. That's how, more or less, how Porter Stansberry ended up owning Porter Stansberry. Mark Ford understood that Porter wanted to be an owner. He knew what he had in terms of copywriting power. He knew what he could produce. And he didn't want to do it for someone else. So if Agora wanted to make money with Porter Stansberry, they were gonna have to give him ownership. You know, and I think if Mark, I told the story wrong at boot camp, and so Mark corrected me, but it was something like they started out with like 25 or 30% of the company, and then it went up each year as Porter was successful. So, the Hispanics people go through to buy a company. It takes over a year, quite often, to do the due diligence and everything else to buy a company. You can end up owning a company or a big chunk of a company, simply by doing your job and again, allowing the client to raise your wages. Letting him know you're in demand. I had this wonderful thing happen, this kind of bragging, but it was fun. At the financial publishers round table in Austin, Texas, two years ago, the whole thing was over. And Martin Weiss, my client and I were sitting on the patio having lunch. And the participants were leaving one by one. And one by one, they stopped by our table. And several of them slapped Martin on the shoulder and said, when are you gonna let Clayton go so we can hire him? I was in contract negotiations at the time. It was a beautiful thing. How nice that man was to me for the next several weeks. So, all right, rule of thumb clients will, there's no brainer. They'd rather pay you nothing or very little on the front end and pay everything else out of the money you make for them. That's just a natural dynamic. If, anytime you lower the front fee, you have a reason to raise the back fee. So, if a guy comes to you and he says, man, I wanna hire you, I just don't have any money to pay a copywriter. I can't pay 12.5 for a copywriter. No problem. If you like the product, if you think there's a lot of potential there, this is a gift from the gods, okay? So you say, no problem, I'll take 30% of revenues and you can pay half my fee up front, or 25% or something like that. It's a seesaw, right? So, yeah, it's especially good when you see potential client doesn't, like J. Abraham seeing Icy Hot, and walking away with 20 million or whatever it was. So, great tool in your toolbox. It's okay to give up income in return for security if that's what you want. And at this point in my life, rather than taking time to beat the bushes or taking time to learn new customers or learn about new markets or new niches or any of that other stuff, I wanna have pastoral life. I wanna go ride my bike on the beach. I wanna have a good life with Wendy now that the kids are gone. And I said, best days in your life is apparent, the day they're born, the day they go to college. And it's just been so true for us. It's been like a honeymoon all over again. But so for me, it was like, I want no hassle, I want plenty of money, I don't have to squeeze every dime out of this deal. And I want some ownership. And you can do that in a lot of ways with a company. You can get a stock option, which basically says that at any point in this period of time or on date certain, you can buy X% of the company at the price of X, the current price of the company. So if you make that company much more valuable, you go, okay, well, when I bought this stock option, okay, the company was only worth a dollar a share, now it's worth $10 a share. And I've got 10,000 shares. So I think I'll go ahead and exercise my right to buy this stock. Yeah. How would you do that if the company's not a publicly traded company, since the family owned a company? It doesn't matter. Carlina, just in case of my question, I'm currently asked how do you do that if it's a family or a privately owned company as opposed to publicly owned? It doesn't matter. If it's a corporation or a limited liability, LLC, there are still shares in the company. And if not, they can create them as part of this contract. They just simply say, okay, well, the company's worth $10 million. And so we are going to issue 1 million shares of stock in this company. And all of my corporations I issued stock in. It's a simple $50 legal thing that they set up. And so then that stock, but there are a couple of types of stock and just keep this in mind. We won't go too far down this road because you should get an attorney involved. But there's three kinds. There's virtual stock, which basically says that as the holder of those shares, you get a certain percentage of the bottom line revenues, the bottom line profits of the company at the end of the year. But you don't have voting interest. There's no survival. You can't sell that stock to anybody else, it's only for you. Then there's real stock where you get the stock. And if you croak, get hit by a truck, make gets the stock or it goes to your kids. It's just part of your estate. So it's there forever, right? If you leave the company, that stock's still good. Virtual stock isn't, it goes away. Virtual stock or phantom stock is a glorified bonus program. Okay? And then there's, and you probably will never get this, but then there's like full voting stock where you actually get a vote at stockholders meetings and so forth. And I had that for a while with Weiss years ago and I shut that down. There's some liability that goes along with that, being on the board of directors and stuff like that. So I didn't want that. So, but if you get involved in that, just get a, don't listen to anything I say, get a lawyer and do what he says. Let's see, yeah, these are the benefits of doing a deal like that. For those of you who are getting started, you're not gonna see this opportunity, but a few years down the road you will. There are a few wrinkles to all of this. The incentives we talked about could be based on gross revenues or they could subtract, they could be gross revenues as total revenues produced by your promotion. Or that it could be based on total revenues minus marketing costs. And it could also be minus 38 refunds. Be very careful when you're looking at this. You do not want online. You do not want your commission to be based on revenues minus marketing costs. Because why? The client's goal is to lose money on your campaign. So he loses money because of the marketing costs. When you get your commission normally, the marketing costs are not deducted. You get that 10% out of the total revenues you generated, right? So if you let him deduct marketing costs, you're never gonna see a royalty. All right, however, minus 30 day refunds, that's cool. Typically, they'll be 10 to 30%. Refunds will be 10 to 30%. I think boardroom is seeing 30% on their health products now. We're seeing 10% in the first 30 days on our financial products. But then over the long haul, our cancellations are much more than boardrooms. So you can get paid based on an increase. So not on the total amount of money you bring in, but rather the increase. And obviously, if that's gonna be what you're paid on, the percentage is much, much, much higher. Because they're deducting everything out of that. And you're just getting that sliver. So if you give them a 30% increase in sales, you're gonna want a big chunk of that 30%. It could be an increase in return on investment. If they have that kind of business model where they're looking at return on investment and wanna maximize it, as opposed to breaking even, as most people do in the mail, you could base a compensation on that. And also decrease in cost per lead. So pretty self-explanatory, I think. Okay, let's just quickly look at some scenarios. All right, so let's say you got a great product, but the client has limited funds. Some options that you could do, okay, incentive only, okay? All right for free, I just want the commission or whatever. Small fee plus incentive, just get some, make sure he's got some skin in the game. Maybe instead of charging you're 12, you charge 2,500 or something. But you remember the seesaw, so your royalty rate goes up. Or a partnership, you say, okay, I'll do this with you, I won't take any money. But you're providing the product, I'm providing the marketing, I say 50-50 split would work. Some guys will go for that. I'm still surprised the guys in Brussels went for 30% of revenue. I mean, they didn't even think about it. So you won't know until you ask, right? Acquisition scenario number two. Now acquisition, we're talking about generating customers or leads, right? From coal lists. Unproven product limited funds, moderate fee. Now you could raise your fee a little bit. Plus a very strong royalty. Or again, you could do a partnership. Great product ample marketing funds. Incentive only, okay? Great product ample marketing funds. It sounds like we could really hit it out of the park. And what does that mean? I mean, if he's got a big list and he's got a great product and he's got plenty of money, then I mean, we've generated, out of a list of 30,000, we once generated $12.5 million. Now, if your incentive is 10% or 20% because you didn't offer an upfront, do the math. You're a millionaire. You're now a millionaire based on one month's work. Retainer plus incentive, self-explanatory. Unproven product, but he's got a lot of money. And here's some of the options that you could have, okay? Another option could be, and I really like this, and I'm enjoying it more and more at the older I get, is to just say, look, you got an unproven product here. I may need more than one bite at the apple. We don't know if this market is gonna love or hate your product. We don't know if the themes are right. I want more than one bite at this apple. Tell you what, I'll do the campaign for you. Based on results, I'll do these test panels, maybe headline test panels or whatever, right? And we'll make this a winner, but it's gonna be a process. It's gonna be a learning process. So, and these are the ways that you might structure a deal with that. Again, you're signing up for a longer haul deal. It's not a permanent partnership. It's not a marriage. It's just saying, look, on this one product, I think we'd be naive to say that we're gonna hit it out on the park on our first try. So what I like to do is to give you several options and to improve the stuff as it goes on and stick with the product. Clients love that stuff. Now, what if they have more than one product? They have an extensive line, okay? I don't think I have this anywhere else, but just a quick story. There's nothing better than getting paid for work you did five years ago. Even if it's a little tiny trickle of income. So, Carleen and I went to Health Resources and we were doing their direct mail. And we said, you need a website. Now, see, with direct mail, it has a life, right? It begins here and then it wears out and it dies. Websites are there forever. And so we negotiated a deal with Lain so that we had a royalty on every product that was sold on his website. But we had to write the entire website. And it wasn't that much work, well, it was for her. I knew you were gonna say that. What do you mean we, what a man? Well, and we wrote the catalog and I did help write the catalog. But anyway, getting continuing royalties on that kind of deal is just amazing. So think about that. We're talking about making a pie bigger, right? You come in and you say, geez, you don't pay very well. But I see you're not only does your marketing suck, your website does too. So let me fix that for you and I'll take a percentage of sales off the website. You don't even have to pay me up front. So that's another way to make the pie bigger. So that was this kind of deal, right? Extensive line of products. So again, you can try these approaches to a deal, see which one works the best for you, see which one the client seems to jump at. Okay, house file promotions go for a small fee, a larger incentive. Again, why? There's no marketing cost and very high response rates. Do you stand to make a lot of money? And it doesn't take too many of those kinds of deals where you have 10 or 20 or 30% of a house file promotion. To a good sized file with a good product. It doesn't take too many of those deals to bring you retirement money. The kind of money that you could retire on. Small customer file, again, does this make sense? Is this intuitive now? Okay, good. Not all customer files are created equal. I think I got into this yesterday a little bit, my lesson with Money Show. We have 400,000 names. Really, when I jumped in, we actually did a promotion, I found out no. They have 400,000 names they can't mail to. They've got 70,000 that they can. And those people don't know who Kim Githler is. They're not bonded to the company. So that's a very different kind of promotion than what I thought it was to begin with. So you need to understand the makeup of the file. Kim's file was a passive file, not an active one. These are people who go to the website to get free information and have given an email address at some point. But okay, when someone gives an email address, that doesn't necessarily mean you can market to that person. There had to have been a disclaimer on the page when they gave you that email address it says, I hereby give you the right to send me additional information, news updates, and special offers from Money Show and its partners. So anyway, enough said, know the file, ask the questions. Okay, any questions? Yes, microphone. When you do a retainer, how do you set up what they expect from you for that retainer? It's just simply a discussion. And there's a lot that goes into it. For example, you're gonna wanna know. And there are questions you can ask that they will feel your fee going up as you ask these questions. Okay, one of them for example is, is this an exclusive? Does this mean I can't write for anybody else? Yes, boy, I hope you can afford this. All right, does this mean I have to be at my desk at certain hours? Or can I work on my own time? No, we really want you there during business hours. Okay, now he's feeling, he knows with each of these answers, you're gonna have to ask him for more money. So each deal is different, and each deal is different based on who you are, who the client is, and what you're being asked to do. But if I was starting out in life, I would be looking at my overhead or how much I wanted to make. I remember, this was a great exercise for me. I sat down and designed my perfect life. Okay? I live in a, this, now this is in the 70s, okay? I live in a $500,000 home on the beach in Redondo. I have an airplane, I have a car, I have this motorcycle, okay? My kids are in a private school, two kids, two two-itions, okay? All of these things are happening, okay? How much money do I need per year to live that perfect life for me? I wanna save this much money every month, okay? I gotta pay my taxes on what I earn. So how much money am I talking about? And believe it or not, at the time, the number was, for my perfect life, was $70,000 a month. I mean a year, a year. And man, I was traveling first class, I had in my own airplane. It was really cool, you know? So I looked at that, 70,000 a year, okay? How can I get from where I am today, which is about 15,000 a year, to the 70,000 a year? So have a goal, know what you're shooting for. If you're not shooting at anything, you're gonna hit it. You know? So know where you wanna be. The proper answer isn't as much as possible. The proper answer is a number, and figure that out. Does that help? I'm a little scattered brain, did I answer your question? Okay, all right, any other questions? Yes? I have a question about lists, because I asked the current project that I'm working on about what kind of lists, and they didn't wanna give me any information. They left out all the answers to those questions. So, I mean, I know it's my first time working with them, so I didn't pursue it, but how would you do that going forward, if they don't wanna give that information? Why do you think they didn't wanna tell you? It's the first time I'm working with them. Maybe they thought you were asking too granular a question. You don't have to ask what specific lists, okay? Okay, now if you're one of these companies, you have spent tens and tens of millions of dollars testing lists over time to see which ones work. Those are the crown jewels, those are the family jewels, right? The last thing, if they have somebody internally who has a list of their lists that are working for them, if that person has ever let go, they will be walked to the door by an arm guard, and they will be searched, right? By then, it's probably too late. They've probably gotten that list out of the company, right? But if that list falls into a competitor's hands, it's an enormous advantage for that competitor, right? Wendy was that person at Phillips Health. Wendy and Carly both did lists. And so when Wendy and I got together, there's a funny story about how they found out. Okay, I called her every morning to see how she was doing and just talking and stuff. And we were getting pretty romantic at that point in time. But before I called her this one morning, I called Richard St. Jones, who's one of the vice presidents there, because I had some business with him. And I asked him my questions and stuff in his voicemail. He wasn't in yet. And so then I hit this thing to disconnect from voicemail, and I punched in her thing. Then we started talking, very intimate conversation, kind of spicy. And then halfway through it, we hear this beep. You know? So Richard had never been to my house in Florida. He was there like the next day. Now, here I am, the guy that wrote Health and Healing, that's pulling this huge response. Here's Wendy, the list goddess, who knows every single list that's responding to it. If the two of us had wanted to at that point, we could have cost Phillips a fortune. We could have simply said, buy, and gone and done our own thing. It's not in my character to do that. It's not in Wendy's. But Richard got this information, went straight to Bob King, who was a president, said, Clayton and Wendy are together, and there's some stuff going on here, and I need to check out what it is. So, but that's how important lists are. You know, it's the crown jewels of the company. Now, to answer your question, you don't have to ask a granular data. You don't have, say, name the list for me. All you have to do is get a general sense of the kinds of lists they're going to. Now, if they're smart, they're going to be going to the list of people who have bought a similar product. The most, okay, this is good marketing nuts and bolts, okay? It's good to know this stuff. If I go out, there are a lot of different kinds of lists. The worst ones are called compile lists. That's where somebody goes out and they make a list of all the doctors in Sarasota. Those doctors have done nothing to get on that list other than to become a doctor. They haven't bought anything. They haven't indicated a desire to buy anything. They just got compiled onto the list by virtue of who they are. It could be a list of businesses. It could be a list of dentists. It could be a list of, you know, whatever, but it's compiled. You can build it out of publicly available information. Those lists go for as little as 10, 15, $20 per thousand, right? The next level, if I'm a marketing director, the next level up would be, and no marketing director in his right mind would ever use that list, unless he's doing like those postcard packs, you know, that kind of stuff. The next level up is people who have filled out a lead card of some kind, indicating an interest in a particular thing, but they didn't actually buy, or they may or may not have actually bought. So that's the next level up. And those leads, those mailing lists, may go for 40, 50, 60 bucks a thousand. Then the next level up for that marketing director is people who have bought a product like his. They've subscribed to a newsletter before. You know, any kind of newsletter. Next level up, they've subscribed to a newsletter in his niche. Next level up, they've subscribed to a letter in his niche within the last 12 months, which is the definition of active customer, right? 12 months. And the final level up is that they bought within the last 90 days. Those are the hotline names. Call them the hotline names. Okay, so, all you need to know is which of those levels he's at. You know? Then if you want to get more data on who you're talking to, you go to the library, you get that standard rate and data service for mailing lists, and you start looking up him and all of his competitors. And it'll give you demographic and some psychographic data on your prospect. It's good stuff, I don't know why I did plan to present that. You sure, you bet. Any other questions? Yes, microphone back. So, I'm referring back to the PowerPoint slide where it talks about am I really the right writer for this job? And, you know, sometimes you get in with a client and things are going well and the money is great and everything just looks good, but you kind of realize, uh-oh, I made a mistake with this guy. And being with the type of loyalty that I have anyways, I don't want to just up and leave and quit, because I know that I'm not going to be able to do that. I know I could make a difference, and what I've done has made a difference, but I'm working with the wrong type of client because he doesn't want to listen and wants to make that quick money really fast. So, you know, that's happened a couple of times, but I mean, what's your advice on making your exit, being part of this small, you know, community of, everybody knows each other in the direct response, direct mail business, how do you make your exit? And studies show that most women won't leave a marriage until they already have something else lined up. I guess it's a pretty good plan. Yeah, me too. Yeah, use your spare time to go ahead and recruit other clients, you know, and, you know, get something lined up. Do you have a non-compete or exclusive relationship? No. Okay, great. No problem. It's a little tougher for me because I do have a non-compete. So even just the act of talking to one of my, one of my clients competitors could be deemed a, you know, violation of contracts. So I have to be very careful how I do that kind of stuff. But if you don't have one, you know, you need to go ahead and do some of the things we talked about this weekend, go out and find yourself a replacement. And once you do, then you can just say, oh, I'm sorry, my time's booked up. You can come up with a nice excuse. Sometimes it's a good way to all of a sudden double your pay too. So then they're not as ugly to work with. Yeah. Yeah. Another question about lists too, Clayton, when you're talking to a client for the first time and you're asking them, you're like that list that you just gave us was for external, but what do you ask them about their house list? Because like you said, you had, they told you, I think their house list was 400,000 names. Would that be right? Mm-hmm, that's right. Well, we've talked at least on two occasions in the last, yesterday and today, we've talked about that thing with the situation I had with the money show. So those are really the questions you want to ask. What's the total size of your house file? How many are opted into marketing? Okay. How many 12 month customers do you have? People who, there's been some buying activity in the last 12 months. Okay. Those are the, and now some companies will have different levels of products like we do. Okay. You can buy a $39 newsletter for me. You can buy a $695 trading service, or you can buy a $2,000 trading service. Okay. So, and we call it FPN, mid-price and FPS. Okay. Financial premium service, financial newsletter and mid-price. And so, you know, if they do have different levels of products in terms of price, you'd like to get counts. And I think it's interesting, you know, that this, it sounds like, oh, that's a number that they'd really guard, they don't care, they don't care. I asked Brian over at Boardroom, I said, give me all your list counts, and he did. I mean, that day, it was no hesitation. So I knew every segment of his file, how many names he has in that segment. And there was purpose for asking it, and as long as there is a purpose, then they should be fine giving you the answer. Anything else? Usually very willing to give you, you know, the general statistics of what was the average paid order, what their average age is, male-female ratio, and those are really the type of important facts you need to know in order to write your package. So, and once you have a more established relationship with them, they'll give you more information. Yeah. Well, think about it, okay? You go to the SRDS, you look that company up, and the SRDS is gonna tell you what their list counts are. So, you know, it's not one of those private, closely held secrets or anything. Other questions? Okay, oh. One more. There's some people in this room that need to go to the bathroom, Chris. All right, me too, so I'll make it quick. The one you're talking about, Larry Edelson's promotion that Golden Silver won, and you were talking about doing the course, the video sales letter basically as a course, was that to a house list, external list? Yes, well it was both. Both. And okay, here's another idea for ya, all right? Somebody hires you to do a campaign to his house list. Okay, fine. As soon as the campaign's done, or even before if you wanted, depending on your relationship with the client, say why don't you let me turn this into an acquisition campaign? You've done the work once. You're gonna get paid when he uses it to his house file, but that campaign has a lifetime, and it's very short for internet. Instead of being five years' control, it could be a one month kind of thing. So why not turn it into an acquisition campaign? So what would that entail? Well you'd have to write some emails or some ads that would drive people to a page that offers them this free course on gold investing. And then you may have to look at the modules to see if any other minor changes need to be made in the modules themselves to make it evergreen. So it's not talking about today's gold price, or it's not talking about something that recently happened. And that's it. Now you've got something that if it works, it can run forever. And you could be making money on the leads and also money on the revenues that it generates. I'm sure you don't have another question. Ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha. We love giving her a hard time.
